relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: After defeat and punishment, Joshua is told to rise without fear toward Ai.
Ai will share Jericho's fate, though its goods may now be taken.
A large force moves by night into ambush.
The hidden soldiers wait close behind the city.
Joshua's visible force will reenact its former defeat.
Ai's confidence will draw its defenders away from home.
The ambush is told to rise into the emptied city.
Capture will be marked by fire.
The hidden force settles between Bethel and Ai while Joshua remains with the people.
At dawn Joshua musters the army with Israel's elders.
The main camp settles north of Ai across the valley.
Another five thousand are named in the western ambush tradition.
The city is surrounded by visible camp and hidden force.
Ai's king sees an apparent repetition of victory and walks into the trap.
Joshua and Israel perform flight toward the wilderness.
The city's defenders abandon protection to pursue.
Ai and Bethel are emptied of men in the narrative.
Joshua's extended javelin becomes the signal toward the city.
The ambush rises, captures Ai, and sets it burning.
Smoke closes one direction while the fleeing army turns from the other.
Israel turns back toward the trapped defenders.
The people of Ai are enclosed between two forces with no escape.
The king alone is taken alive for later execution.
Israel kills those in field and wilderness, then returns to strike the city.
Twelve thousand men and women are named as dead.
Joshua's arm remains extended until every inhabitant is devoted to destruction.
Animals and goods become lawful spoil for Israel.
Ai becomes a burned heap marked as permanent desolation.
Its king is hanged, removed by sunset, and buried beneath stones at the gate.
The narrative turns from battlefield to altar on Mount Ebal.
Uncut stones receive offerings of surrender and well-being.
Torah is copied onto stone in the sight of Israel.
Leaders and ordinary residents, native-born and immigrant, stand together around the ark.
Blessing and curse are read without omission.
Women, children, and sojourners hear every commanded word.

contemplative_note: Joshua 8 moves from an annihilated city to a fully inclusive Torah assembly. The second scene cannot absolve the first. Its ethical demand is to keep both visible: mourn the people erased by conquest, and preserve the radical public access in which women, children, immigrants, leaders, and laborers all hear the same Torah.
